section.title: Christ the Wisdom and Power of God
section.sectionRationale: Here Paul answers the whole Corinthian obsession with wisdom and status by unfolding the paradox of the cross. The message of a crucified Messiah looks like folly and weakness to the world, yet it is the very wisdom and power of God. God deliberately overturns human standards, saving through what the world despises and choosing the lowly, so that no one may boast before Him. The section climaxes in Christ Himself, who has become for believers wisdom from God: righteousness, holiness, and redemption, leaving one boast alone, boasting in the Lord.
section.thesis: The word of the cross, foolishness and weakness to the perishing, is the wisdom and power of God to the saved; God overturns worldly wisdom and status, choosing the foolish and weak and lowly so that no flesh may boast, for Christ Himself has become our wisdom, righteousness, holiness, and redemption.
section.frameNote: Four movements: the word of the cross, foolishness or power; Christ crucified as the wisdom and power of God to the called; God's choice of the foolish and weak so none may boast; and Christ our wisdom, so that boasting is only in the Lord.

units.label: The word of the cross
units.note: foolishness to the perishing, power to the saved

verses.text: For the message of the cross is {{moria|foolishness}} to those who are perishing, but to us who are being saved it is the {{dynamis|power}} of God.
For it is written: “I will destroy the wisdom of the wise; the intelligence of the intelligent I will frustrate.”
Where is the wise man? Where is the scribe? Where is the philosopher of this age? Has not God made foolish the wisdom of the world?

units.label: Christ crucified, God's wisdom and power
units.note: a stumbling block and folly, yet the wisdom of God

verses.text: For since in the wisdom of God the world through its wisdom did not know Him, God was pleased through the foolishness of what was preached to save those who believe.
Jews demand signs and Greeks search for wisdom,
but we preach Christ crucified, a stumbling block to Jews and foolishness to Gentiles,
but to those who are called, both Jews and Greeks, Christ the power of God and the {{sophia|wisdom}} of God.
For the foolishness of God is wiser than man’s wisdom, and the weakness of God is stronger than man’s strength.

units.label: God chose the foolish and weak
units.note: so that no one may boast

verses.text: Brothers, consider the time of your calling: Not many of you were wise by human standards; not many were powerful; not many were of noble birth.
But God chose the foolish things of the world to shame the wise; God chose the weak things of the world to shame the strong.
He chose the lowly and despised things of the world, and the things that are not, to nullify the things that are,
so that no one may boast in His presence.
It is because of Him that you are in Christ Jesus, who has become for us wisdom from God: our righteousness, holiness, and {{apolytrosis|redemption}}.
Therefore, as it is written: “Let him who boasts {{kauchaomai|boast}} in the Lord.”

moria.gloss: Foolishness, absurdity, the quality of being ridiculous. It is how the message of a crucified Messiah appears to the perishing, and the word from which we get moron. Paul takes the world's dismissive verdict and turns it on its head: this apparent folly is the saving wisdom of God, and God has made the world's own wisdom foolish.

dynamis.gloss: Power, might, effective force, the root of dynamite. The cross, which looks like weakness and defeat, is in fact the power of God that saves. Paul insists that real divine power is displayed not in worldly strength but in the crucified Christ, whose apparent weakness is stronger than all human strength.

sophia.gloss: Wisdom, skill, insight. The Greeks prized it and the Corinthians craved it, yet the world's wisdom failed to know God. In answer, Christ crucified is the true wisdom of God, and Christ Himself becomes wisdom from God for His people, exposing human cleverness as no path to God at all.

kauchaomai.gloss: To boast, to glory, to take pride in. God has arranged salvation so that boasting in oneself is excluded; the only legitimate boast is in the Lord. The Corinthian pride in wisdom, status, and favorite teachers is undone, and every ground of self-congratulation is transferred to Christ.

apolytrosis.gloss: Redemption, release secured by the payment of a ransom, the buying back of a slave or captive. Christ has become our redemption, freeing us at the cost of Himself. It joins righteousness and holiness as one of the saving benefits Christ has become for His people, so that all of salvation is found in Him and not in human achievement.

basic.context: Paul now explains why he refuses to rely on clever words. The message of the cross seems like foolishness to those perishing, but to the saved it is the power of God. God deliberately chose to save through what the world calls weak and foolish, a crucified Messiah, so that human wisdom and pride are overturned. He even chose lowly, unimpressive people, like most of the Corinthians, so that no one could boast. In the end, Christ Himself becomes everything for them, their wisdom, righteousness, holiness, and redemption.
basic.hermeneutics: This is sustained theological argument built on the paradox of the cross and supported by Scripture, Isaiah 29 and Jeremiah 9. Paul reasons from what God did at the cross and from whom God called. The abiding truth is that salvation is God's wise and powerful work, received by faith, so that all boasting is excluded and Christ alone is glorified.
basic.originalLanguages: The cross looks like moria, foolishness, but is the dynamis, power, of God. The world's sophia, wisdom, could not find God, so God saves through what seems foolish. Christ becomes wisdom from God for us, our righteousness, holiness, and apolytrosis, redemption. The result is that the only kauchaomai, boasting, left is boasting in the Lord.
basic.typology: Christ crucified fulfills the pattern of God saving through weakness and reversal seen throughout the Old Testament, choosing the younger, the barren, the small, and the despised. Isaiah's promise to confound the wise is fulfilled at the cross. The redemption Christ becomes fulfills the ransom and release pictured in the exodus and the year of release.
basic.christ: Christ crucified is the center: a stumbling block to Jews, folly to Gentiles, but to the called the power and wisdom of God. More than that, Christ has become for believers wisdom from God, and in Him righteousness, holiness, and redemption. He does not merely give these gifts; He is them for His people.
basic.god: God is sovereign and wise beyond human measure. He deliberately overturns the world's wisdom and strength, saving through the cross and choosing the lowly, precisely so that no one can boast before Him. His foolishness is wiser than men and His weakness stronger than men, revealing a God who saves in a way that humbles all pride.
basic.crossReferences: Isaiah 29 promises God will destroy the wisdom of the wise. Romans says the gospel is God's power for salvation. Second Corinthians shows God's power made perfect in weakness. Jeremiah supplies the call to boast only in knowing the Lord.
basic.oneStory: Throughout the Bible God saves through reversal, lifting the lowly and confounding the proud. The cross is the climax of that pattern: the ultimate weakness that is the ultimate power, the apparent folly that is the deepest wisdom. In Christ crucified, God undoes human boasting and becomes everything His people need.

context.literary: This section develops the theme announced in verse 17, the cross and human eloquence. It unfolds in a tight argument: the paradox of the cross (18 to 25), supported by the Corinthians' own lowly calling (26 to 29), and clinched by Christ as their all-in-all (30 to 31). Two Scripture citations frame it, Isaiah at the start and the call to boast in the Lord at the end.
context.historical: Greek culture idolized rhetoric and philosophy; Jewish expectation looked for signs and a triumphant deliverer. A crucified Messiah offended both: crucifixion was the most shameful of deaths, and a suffering deliverer contradicted Jewish hopes. Corinth's love of status and cleverness made this message especially scandalous, which is exactly Paul's point.
context.canonical: The passage gathers the Old Testament theme of God confounding the wise and exalting the lowly and focuses it on the cross. It stands with Romans 1 and 2 Corinthians on the power of the gospel through weakness, and it anchors the New Testament's insistence that salvation is by grace, so that boasting is excluded.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To dismantle the Corinthians' pride in worldly wisdom and status by showing that God saves through the cross, which overturns all human standards, and to lead them to boast in Christ alone, who has become their wisdom, righteousness, holiness, and redemption.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: Paul describes what God has done in the cross and in calling the lowly; the implied command is to abandon self-boasting and glory in the Lord. Verse 31, boast in the Lord, is the enduring prescription that the whole argument drives toward.

debates.question: What does Paul mean by the foolishness and weakness of God in verse 25?

views.label: The cross as God's chosen means
views.case: God is not actually foolish or weak; Paul speaks of how the cross appears to human eyes. What the world judges as folly and weakness in the crucified Christ is in truth wiser and stronger than anything human, so the phrase is ironic praise of the cross.

views.label: A reversal of human categories
views.case: God deliberately works in a way that inverts human notions of wisdom and power, so that what looks least impressive accomplishes the greatest salvation, exposing the poverty of human standards.

debates.bearing: Both guard against any thought of real deficiency in God; the cross reveals a wisdom and power that surpass and humble every human measure.

debates.question: Does verse 21 mean human reason can never know anything true about God?

views.label: Fallen wisdom cannot save or truly know God
views.case: Paul's point is that the world, left to its own wisdom, failed to come to a saving knowledge of God and needs the gospel; it addresses the limits of wisdom for salvation, not every possible knowledge.

views.label: General revelation leaves people without excuse but not saved
views.case: Read with Romans 1, creation testifies to God so that people are without excuse, yet this witness does not bring saving knowledge; only the preached cross does that.

debates.bearing: The orthodox reading affirms that God is truly revealed in creation, yet holds that saving knowledge of God comes only through the cross, not through human wisdom.

study.originalLanguages: Paul turns the world's insult into praise. The cross is moria, foolishness, to the perishing, yet it is the dynamis, power, of God to the saved. Human sophia, wisdom, so prized in Corinth, could not find God, so God saves through what looks foolish, and Christ crucified is Himself the wisdom and power of God. The climax names Christ as wisdom from God for us, and in Him righteousness, holiness, and apolytrosis, redemption, the ransom that buys back captives. The aim is that no kauchaomai, no boasting, stands before God, leaving only the boast that glories in the Lord.

typology.fulfillment: Fulfills God's repeated pattern of choosing the small, the barren, and the despised; the cross is the ultimate reversal, weakness that saves and folly that is the wisdom of God.

typology.type: The wisdom of the wise confounded, Isaiah 29:14
typology.fulfillment: God's promise to destroy the wisdom of the wise is fulfilled when the cross empties human wisdom of its claim to reach God.
Christ becomes our redemption, fulfilling the exodus deliverance and the release of captives, freeing His people at the cost of Himself.

study.christ: Christ crucified is the theme and the treasure of the passage. To the world He is a scandal and a joke, a Messiah on a Roman cross, but to those God calls He is the power of God and the wisdom of God. Paul then presses further: Christ does not merely provide salvation, He becomes it. He is made for us wisdom from God, and in Him we have righteousness, our standing before God; holiness, our being set apart; and redemption, our release from bondage. Every blessing the Corinthians sought in wisdom and status is found in the crucified and risen Christ alone.
study.god: God is revealed as sovereignly and surprisingly wise. He was pleased to save through the foolishness of preaching, to choose the lowly and despised, and to overturn the wisdom and strength of the world, all with a deliberate purpose: that no one may boast in His presence. His so-called foolishness outstrips human wisdom and His so-called weakness overpowers human strength. This is a God who humbles pride and gives grace, so that all glory returns to Him.

crossReferences.note: The Scripture Paul cites in verse 19: God will destroy the wisdom of the wise, fulfilled in the cross.
The gospel is the power of God for salvation to everyone who believes, matching the power of the cross in verse 18.
God's power is made perfect in weakness, the same paradox as the weakness of God being stronger than men.
The source of verse 31: let the one who boasts boast in knowing the Lord, not in wisdom, might, or riches.

study.oneStory: The Bible tells of a God who saves by reversal, casting down the proud and lifting the lowly. The cross is the summit of that story: the deepest apparent weakness proves the greatest power, and the seeming folly is the wisdom of God for the salvation of the world. In Christ crucified, God empties human boasting and becomes Himself the wisdom, righteousness, holiness, and redemption of His people, so that every knee bows and every boast is in the Lord.

quiz.q: How does Paul describe the message of the cross in verse 18?

options.text: Foolishness to those perishing, but the power of God to those being saved.
options.note: The cross divides hearers: folly to some, saving power to others.

options.text: A wise philosophy that anyone can appreciate.
options.note: It is God's wisdom, but the world judges it folly, not an admired philosophy.

options.text: A message meant only for the educated elite.
options.note: The opposite; God chose the lowly, and the wise stumble over it.

options.text: A powerless story that saves no one.
options.note: To the saved it is the very power of God.

quiz.q: Why did God choose to save through what looks foolish and weak?

options.text: So that no one may boast in His presence, but only in the Lord.
options.note: Verses 29 to 31 give the purpose: to exclude human boasting and glorify God.

options.text: Because God could not find a more impressive way.
options.note: It is a deliberate, wise choice, not a limitation; His weakness is stronger than men.

options.text: To reward those who were already wise and powerful.
options.note: He chose the foolish and weak to shame the wise and strong.

options.text: Because appearances do not matter to God at all.
options.note: The point is a purposeful reversal to humble pride, not indifference.

quiz.q: How do Jews and Greeks respond to the preaching of Christ crucified?

options.text: It is a stumbling block to Jews and foolishness to Gentiles, but God's power and wisdom to the called.
options.note: Verses 22 to 24 capture both offenses and the saving reality for the called.

options.text: Both groups immediately welcome it as obvious truth.
options.note: Only those God calls receive it; to the rest it is offense or folly.

options.text: Only the Jews reject it, while all Greeks accept it.
options.note: Paul says it is folly to Gentiles and a stumbling block to Jews alike.

options.text: Everyone rejects it, and no one is saved.
options.note: To those who are called it is the power and wisdom of God.

quiz.q: What has Christ become for believers according to verse 30?

options.text: Wisdom from God: their righteousness, holiness, and redemption.
options.note: Christ is not merely the giver but the substance of every saving benefit.

options.text: A wise teacher who shows them how to save themselves.
options.note: He does not merely instruct; He becomes their righteousness and redemption.

options.text: One helpful source of wisdom among many others.
options.note: He is wisdom from God, the sole ground of their standing and freedom.

options.text: An example to admire but not a Savior to trust.
options.note: He is their redemption, purchased at the cost of Himself.

quiz.q: What is the one legitimate boast Paul leaves standing?

options.text: Boasting in the Lord.
options.note: Verse 31 cites Jeremiah: let the one who boasts boast in the Lord.

options.text: Boasting in the leader who taught you the most.
options.note: That is the very party pride Paul dismantles; the boast belongs to the Lord.

options.text: Boasting in your spiritual gifts and knowledge.
options.note: These are gifts of grace, not grounds for boasting before God.

options.text: Boasting in your wisdom and social standing.
options.note: God chose the lowly precisely so that such boasting is excluded.

questions: The cross looks like foolishness to the world but is the power of God. Where are you tempted to be embarrassed by the plain message of a crucified Savior?
God chose the lowly and unimpressive on purpose. How does it comfort you, and humble you, to know you were not chosen for your wisdom, strength, or status?
Christ has become your righteousness, holiness, and redemption. Which of these do you most need to rest in rather than trying to achieve it yourself?
Paul leaves only one boast standing, boasting in the Lord. What are you quietly boasting in instead, and what would it look like to transfer that boast to Christ?
The world still prizes cleverness and strength. How can you value the wisdom of the cross above the wisdom of the age in the decisions you make this week?

translationNotes.note: This quotes Isaiah 29:14, where God promises to confound the wisdom of the wise; Paul applies it to the way the cross empties human wisdom of its claim to reach God.
Christ crucified is a stumbling block, the Greek skandalon, to Jews; the shame of crucifixion and a suffering Messiah offended Jewish expectations of a triumphant deliverer.
The list righteousness, holiness, and redemption unpacks how Christ is wisdom from God; He is not only the giver of these but has become them for His people.
This condenses Jeremiah 9:23-24, where boasting in wisdom, might, and riches is forbidden and boasting in knowing the Lord is commended.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for 1 Corinthians 1:18-31. Any em dashes in the source have been rendered as commas per the no-dash style rule; wording is otherwise unchanged.
